<!doctype html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
<meta charset="utf-8" />
<meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width,initial-scale=1" />
<title>Extremal combinatorics — Turán, Ramsey, Sperner, removal</title>
<link rel="stylesheet" href="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/katex@0.16.11/dist/katex.min.css">
<script defer src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/katex@0.16.11/dist/katex.min.js"></script>
<script defer src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/katex@0.16.11/dist/contrib/auto-render.min.js"></script>
<script>
(function(){
  function renderAll(){
    if(typeof renderMathInElement!=='function'){ return setTimeout(renderAll,50); }
    renderMathInElement(document.body,{
      delimiters:[
        {left:'$$',right:'$$',display:true},
        {left:'$',right:'$',display:false},
        {left:'\\(',right:'\\)',display:false},
        {left:'\\[',right:'\\]',display:true}
      ],
      throwOnError:false,
      macros:{
        '\\Spec':'\\Spec',
        '\\Gal':'\\Gal',
        '\\Hom':'\\Hom',
        '\\tr':'\\tr',
        '\\ad':'\\ad',
        '\\ind':'\\ind'
      }
    });
  }
  if(document.readyState==='loading') document.addEventListener('DOMContentLoaded',renderAll);
  else renderAll();
})();
</script>
<style>
  :root{
    --bg:#0f1218; --panel:#161b24; --panel2:#1c2230;
    --ink:#e8ecf1; --mute:#9aa4b2; --line:#2a3242;
    --yellow:#ffd866; --blue:#58c4dd; --green:#83c167;
    --pink:#e07a5f; --violet:#b39ddb; --cyan:#7de0d6;
  }
  *{box-sizing:border-box}
  html,body{margin:0;padding:0;background:var(--bg);color:var(--ink);
    font-family:-apple-system,BlinkMacSystemFont,"Segoe UI",Inter,Roboto,sans-serif;
    font-size:17px;line-height:1.65}
  a{color:var(--blue)}
  code{font-family:ui-monospace,SFMono-Regular,Menlo,Consolas,monospace;
    background:#0b0f16;padding:1px 6px;border-radius:4px;color:#f0c987;font-size:0.92em}
  main{max-width:880px;margin:0 auto;padding:4rem 1.5rem 8rem}
  h1{font-size:2.4rem;letter-spacing:-0.01em;margin:0 0 0.4rem}
  h2{font-size:1.6rem;margin:3.2rem 0 0.8rem;color:#fff;border-bottom:1px solid var(--line);padding-bottom:.4rem}
  h3{font-size:1.15rem;margin:1.8rem 0 .6rem;color:var(--yellow)}
  p{margin:0.7rem 0}
  .sub{color:var(--mute);margin-top:0;margin-bottom:2rem}
  .widget{background:var(--panel);border:1px solid var(--line);border-radius:10px;
    padding:1rem 1.1rem 1.2rem;margin:1.2rem 0}
  .widget .hd{display:flex;justify-content:space-between;align-items:baseline;margin-bottom:.6rem;gap:.8rem;flex-wrap:wrap}
  .widget .hd .ttl{font-weight:600;color:#fff;font-size:.95rem;letter-spacing:.02em;text-transform:uppercase}
  .widget .hd .hint{color:var(--mute);font-size:.85rem}
  .row{display:flex;gap:.6rem;flex-wrap:wrap;align-items:center;margin:.4rem 0}
  .row label{color:var(--mute);font-size:.88rem}
  input[type="text"],input[type="number"],select{
    background:#0b0f16;color:var(--ink);border:1px solid var(--line);
    border-radius:6px;padding:.35rem .55rem;font-family:inherit;font-size:.95rem}
  input[type="range"]{accent-color:var(--yellow);flex:1;max-width:260px}
  button{background:var(--panel2);color:var(--ink);border:1px solid var(--line);
    border-radius:6px;padding:.35rem .7rem;cursor:pointer;font:inherit}
  button:hover{background:#242c3e}
  button.active{background:var(--yellow);color:#111;border-color:var(--yellow)}
  .readout{background:#0b0f16;border:1px solid var(--line);border-radius:6px;
    padding:.6rem .8rem;font-family:ui-monospace,monospace;font-size:.92rem;color:#cbd5e1;
    min-height:1.6em;white-space:pre-wrap;word-break:break-word}
  .pill{display:inline-block;background:var(--panel2);border:1px solid var(--line);
    border-radius:999px;padding:1px 8px;font-size:.82rem;color:var(--mute);margin-right:4px}
  .note{border-left:3px solid var(--violet);padding:.3rem .9rem;color:#d8d2ef;background:#1a1828;
    margin:1rem 0;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0}
  .ok{border-left:3px solid var(--green);padding:.3rem .9rem;color:#d5ebc8;background:#1a2418;
    margin:1rem 0;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0}
  .bad{border-left:3px solid var(--pink);padding:.3rem .9rem;color:#f4d2c7;background:#2a1a18;
    margin:1rem 0;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0}
  svg{display:block;margin:0 auto;max-width:100%}
  svg text{fill:var(--ink);font-family:inherit}
  nav.toc{position:sticky;top:0;background:rgba(15,18,24,.93);backdrop-filter:blur(8px);
    border-bottom:1px solid var(--line);padding:.6rem 1rem;z-index:10;
    display:flex;gap:1rem;flex-wrap:wrap;font-size:.9rem}
  nav.toc a{color:var(--mute);text-decoration:none}
  nav.toc a:hover{color:var(--yellow)}
  /* sticky sidebar TOC (wide screens only; narrow screens fall back to top-nav) */
  aside.sidetoc{position:fixed;top:4.2rem;
    left:max(1rem, calc(50% - 440px - 220px));
    width:200px;max-height:calc(100vh - 6rem);overflow-y:auto;
    padding-right:1rem;border-right:1px solid var(--line);
    font-size:.88rem;z-index:5}
  aside.sidetoc .sidetoc-title{font-size:.7rem;letter-spacing:.14em;text-transform:uppercase;
    color:var(--mute);margin:0 0 .5rem;padding-bottom:.35rem;border-bottom:1px solid var(--line)}
  aside.sidetoc ol{list-style:none;padding:0;margin:0}
  aside.sidetoc li{margin:0}
  aside.sidetoc a{display:block;padding:.3rem .5rem;color:var(--mute);text-decoration:none;
    border-left:2px solid transparent;line-height:1.35;
    transition:color 120ms ease, border-color 120ms ease}
  aside.sidetoc a:hover{color:var(--ink)}
  aside.sidetoc a.active{color:var(--yellow);border-left-color:var(--yellow)}
  @media (max-width:1180px){ aside.sidetoc{display:none} }
  @media (min-width:1181px){ nav.toc a[href^="#"]{display:none} }
  .hero{padding:3rem 0 1rem}
  .small{font-size:.88rem;color:var(--mute)}
  footer{color:var(--mute);font-size:.88rem;text-align:center;margin-top:4rem}
  table.plain{width:100%;border-collapse:collapse;font-size:.95rem;margin:.6rem 0}
  table.plain th,table.plain td{padding:.35rem .5rem;border-bottom:1px solid var(--line);text-align:left}
  table.plain th{color:var(--mute);font-weight:500}
  /* callback-css-auto-begin */
  aside.callback{
    margin:1.2rem 0;padding:.7rem 1rem;
    background:rgba(88,196,221,0.05);
    border-left:3px solid color-mix(in srgb, var(--cyan) 45%, transparent);
    border-radius:0 6px 6px 0;
    font-size:.93rem;
  }
  aside.callback .ttl{
    font-size:.68rem;letter-spacing:.14em;text-transform:uppercase;
    color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);margin-bottom:.3rem;font-weight:600;
  }
  aside.callback ul{margin:0;padding-left:1.1rem}
  aside.callback ul li{margin:.15rem 0}
  aside.callback a{color:inherit}
/* callback-css-auto-end */
  details.changelog{
    margin:2rem 0 1rem;padding:.6rem .9rem;
    background:rgba(0,0,0,0.22);
    border-radius:6px;
    font-size:.85rem;
  }
  details.changelog summary{
    cursor:pointer;letter-spacing:.1em;text-transform:uppercase;
    font-size:.7rem;color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);
  }
  details.changelog table{margin-top:.5rem;border-collapse:collapse;width:100%;font-size:.82rem}
  details.changelog td{padding:.2rem .6rem;border-bottom:1px dashed rgba(255,255,255,0.07);vertical-align:top}
  details.changelog td:first-child{color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);white-space:nowrap;font-variant-numeric:tabular-nums}
  aside.related{
    margin:1.2rem 0;padding:.7rem 1rem;
    background:color-mix(in srgb, var(--mute) 6%, transparent);
    border-left:3px solid color-mix(in srgb, var(--mute) 55%, transparent);
    border-radius:0 6px 6px 0;
    font-size:.93rem;
  }
  aside.related .ttl{
    font-size:.68rem;letter-spacing:.14em;text-transform:uppercase;
    color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);margin-bottom:.3rem;font-weight:600;
  }
  aside.related > div{margin:.15rem 0}
  aside.related .more{color:var(--mute);font-style:italic}
  aside.related a{color:inherit}
  /* display-prefs-css-auto-begin */
  html[data-hide-widgets] .widget { display: none !important; }
  html[data-hide-quizzes] .quiz { display: none !important; }
  html[data-hide-lineage] .lineage-strip { display: none !important; }
  .mv-display-toggles { display: inline-flex; gap: .6rem; align-items: center; }
  .mv-display-toggle { background: transparent; border: 1px solid var(--line, #333); border-radius: 6px; padding: 5px 10px; cursor: pointer; font-size: 1.05rem; line-height: 1; color: inherit; transition: background 120ms ease, border-color 120ms ease; }
  .mv-display-toggle:hover { background: var(--panel, rgba(255,255,255,0.06)); border-color: var(--mute, #666); }
  .mv-display-toggle--off { opacity: 0.35; }
  /* display-prefs-css-auto-end */
</style>

Extremal combinatorics

How dense can a graph be before it must contain a triangle? How many edges force a $K_{2,2}$? How big an antichain fits in the Boolean lattice? Extremal combinatorics asks "what is the maximum size of a structure avoiding a forbidden pattern?" — and answers in sharp asymptotics. The five landmark theorems on this page (Turán, Kővári–Sós–Turán, Erdős–Stone–Simonovits, Ramsey, Sperner) plus the removal-lemma machinery sit at the centre of the field; together they exhibit a recurring move where a counting inequality compresses local restrictions into a global density bound.

blocks.html:
1. Turán's theorem

The founding result of extremal graph theory. Fix integers $r \ge 1$ and $n \ge 1$. The Turán number $\mathrm{ex}(n, K_{r+1})$ is the maximum number of edges in a simple graph on $n$ vertices that contains no copy of the complete graph $K_{r+1}$. Turán (1941) determined it exactly.

Theorem (Turán, 1941). For all $n, r \ge 1$, $$\mathrm{ex}(n, K_{r+1}) \;=\; \Big(1 - \tfrac{1}{r}\Big)\,\frac{n^2}{2} \;-\; O(1),$$ and the unique extremal graph is the Turán graph $T(n, r)$ — the complete $r$-partite graph on $n$ vertices with parts of sizes as equal as possible.

The Turán graph $T(n, r)$

Partition $\{1, \ldots, n\}$ into $r$ parts $V_1, \ldots, V_r$ with $|V_i| \in \{\lfloor n/r \rfloor, \lceil n/r \rceil\}$. Connect $u$ to $v$ iff they lie in different parts. So $T(n, r)$ has no independent set of size $r+1$ inside any single part — actually it has no $K_{r+1}$ at all, because $K_{r+1}$ would require $r+1$ vertices pairwise adjacent, hence in $r+1$ distinct parts, contradiction. The number of edges is $$e(T(n, r)) \;=\; \binom{n}{2} - \sum_{i=1}^{r} \binom{|V_i|}{2}.$$ When $r \mid n$ this simplifies to $\frac{r-1}{r} \cdot \frac{n^2}{2}$, the limiting density.

Proof idea — vertex-shifting / weight-pushing

Among all $K_{r+1}$-free graphs on $n$ vertices with the maximum number of edges, take one whose largest independent neighbourhood is as large as possible. A short argument shows that for non-adjacent $u, v$ with $\deg(u) \ge \deg(v)$, replacing $v$ by a copy of $u$ does not decrease the edge count and preserves $K_{r+1}$-freeness. Iterating produces a complete multi-partite graph; balancing the parts maximizes edges by convexity. This is sometimes called Zykov symmetrization.

The "right" parameter is density. Dividing both sides by $\binom{n}{2}$, Turán's theorem reads $\mathrm{ex}(n, K_{r+1}) / \binom{n}{2} \to 1 - 1/r$ as $n \to \infty$. The number $1 - 1/r$ is the Turán density $\pi(K_{r+1})$. It depends only on the chromatic number of the forbidden graph — a foreshadowing of Erdős–Stone–Simonovits in § 3.

Turán graph $T(n, r)$
drag the sliders to see how parts and edges scale
n = 9 r = 3
Vertices are split into $r$ parts (each part one colour); every edge runs between different parts. The graph is $K_{r+1}$-free because any clique of size $r+1$ would need $r+1$ pairwise-different parts. The dashed-line target shows the limiting edge count $(1-1/r)\,n^2/2$.

<script>
/* ============================ widget 1: Turán graph ============================ */
(function(){
  const nIn = $('#turan-n'), rIn = $('#turan-r');
  const nv = $('#turan-nv'), rv = $('#turan-rv');
  const svg = $('#turan-svg'), out = $('#turan-out');
  const palette = ['var(--yellow)','var(--blue)','var(--green)','var(--pink)','var(--violet)','var(--cyan)'];

  function partition(n, r){
    const parts = Array.from({length:r}, ()=>[]);
    for(let i=0;i<n;i++) parts[i % r].push(i);
    return parts;
  }
  function edgeCount(parts){
    const total = parts.reduce((s,p)=>s+p.length,0);
    let same = 0;
    for(const p of parts) same += p.length*(p.length-1)/2;
    return total*(total-1)/2 - same;
  }
  function render(){
    const n = parseInt(nIn.value,10);
    const r = Math.min(parseInt(rIn.value,10), n);
    rIn.max = String(Math.min(6, n));
    if(parseInt(rIn.value,10) > n){ rIn.value = String(n); }
    nv.textContent = `n = ${n}`;
    rv.textContent = `r = ${r}`;

    const parts = partition(n, r);
    const W = 620, H = 360, cx = W/2, cy = H/2 - 10, R = Math.min(W,H)*0.36;

    svg.innerHTML = '';
    // place vertices on a circle, grouped by part (so part-arcs stay together)
    const order = [];
    for(let i=0;i<r;i++) for(const v of parts[i]) order.push({v, part:i});
    const pos = new Map();
    order.forEach((o, idx)=>{
      const t = -Math.PI/2 + 2*Math.PI*idx/n;
      pos.set(o.v, {x: cx + R*Math.cos(t), y: cy + R*Math.sin(t), part: o.part});
    });
    // edges
    for(let i=0;i<n;i++) for(let j=i+1;j<n;j++){
      const pi = pos.get(i), pj = pos.get(j);
      if(pi.part === pj.part) continue;
      svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:pi.x,y1:pi.y,x2:pj.x,y2:pj.y, stroke:'var(--line)', 'stroke-width':1, opacity:0.55}));
    }
    // vertices
    for(let i=0;i<n;i++){
      const p = pos.get(i);
      svg.appendChild(SVG('circle',{cx:p.x, cy:p.y, r:9, fill:palette[p.part % palette.length], stroke:'var(--panel2)', 'stroke-width':1.5}));
    }
    // legend / readout
    const e = edgeCount(parts);
    const limit = (1 - 1/r) * n*n/2;
    const denom = n*(n-1)/2;
    const sizes = parts.map(p=>p.length).join(' + ');
    out.textContent =
      `n = ${n},  r = ${r}\n` +
      `parts: ${sizes}    (sum = ${n})\n` +
      `e(T(n,r))   = ${e}    (= edges between distinct parts)\n` +
      `density e/(n choose 2) = ${(e/denom).toFixed(4)}    target 1 − 1/r = ${(1-1/r).toFixed(4)}\n` +
      `limiting (1−1/r)·n²/2 = ${limit.toFixed(2)}    Turán bound on n=${n} vertices`;
    // top label
    const lbl = SVG('text', {x:cx, y:H-12, 'text-anchor':'middle','font-size':12, fill:'var(--mute)'});
    lbl.textContent = `T(${n}, ${r}) — ${e} edges, ${r} colour classes`;
    svg.appendChild(lbl);
  }
  nIn.addEventListener('input', render);
  rIn.addEventListener('input', render);
  render();
})();
</script>
</section>

2. Kővári–Sós–Turán & the Zarankiewicz problem

Turán's theorem handles forbidden complete graphs $K_{r+1}$, all of which have chromatic number $\ge 3$. The bipartite case ($\chi = 2$) is fundamentally different: forbidding $K_{2,2}$ allows only $O(n^{3/2})$ edges, not $\Theta(n^2)$. The first general bound is Kővári–Sós–Turán (1954).

Theorem (KST). For $2 \le s \le t$, $$\mathrm{ex}(n, K_{s,t}) \;\le\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,(t-1)^{1/s}\,(n - s + 1)\,n^{1 - 1/s} + \tfrac{s-1}{2}\,n.$$ In particular, $\mathrm{ex}(n, K_{s,t}) = O(n^{2 - 1/s})$.

Proof — double counting cherries

Let $G$ be a $K_{s,t}$-free graph on $n$ vertices with $m$ edges. Count pairs $(v, S)$ where $v \in V(G)$, $S \subseteq N(v)$, and $|S| = s$. On one side this equals $\sum_v \binom{\deg(v)}{s}$; by convexity (Jensen) this is at least $n \binom{2m/n}{s}$. On the other side, each $s$-subset $S$ of vertices is the neighbourhood of at most $t-1$ vertices (else $S$ has $t$ common neighbours, forming $K_{s,t}$). So $\sum_v \binom{\deg(v)}{s} \le (t-1) \binom{n}{s}$. Solving for $m$ gives the stated bound.

The Zarankiewicz problem

The bipartite version: $z(m, n; s, t)$ is the maximum number of $1$s in an $m \times n$ $0/1$-matrix with no $s \times t$ all-ones sub-matrix. Equivalently, maximum edges in a bipartite graph $G \subseteq K_{m, n}$ with no $K_{s, t}$. KST gives $z(n, n; 2, 2) = O(n^{3/2})$; the constant is sharp via the Erdős–Rényi polarity construction: vertices are points and lines of a projective plane $\mathrm{PG}(2, q)$, edges given by incidence. This achieves $\Theta(n^{3/2})$, matching KST up to constants.

$K_{r+1}$$r+1$$\sim (1 - 1/r)\,n^2/2$$T(n, r)$

KST exponent $2 - 1/s$
slide $s$ to see the bound shrink toward $n^2$ but never reach it
s = 2 t = 2
$\mathrm{ex}(n, K_{s,t}) \le \tfrac{1}{2}(t-1)^{1/s} n^{2-1/s} + O(n)$. As $s\to\infty$, the exponent $2-1/s$ approaches $2$ — the bound for forbidden bipartite graphs is always strictly subquadratic but can be arbitrarily close to it.

/* ============================ widget 2: KST exponent ============================ */
(function(){
  const sIn = $('#kst-s'), tIn = $('#kst-t');
  const sv = $('#kst-sv'), tv = $('#kst-tv');
  const svg = $('#kst-svg'), out = $('#kst-out');

  function render(){
    const s = parseInt(sIn.value,10);
    const t = Math.max(s, parseInt(tIn.value,10));
    tIn.min = String(s);
    if(parseInt(tIn.value,10) < s){ tIn.value = String(s); }
    sv.textContent = `s = ${s}`;
    tv.textContent = `t = ${t}`;

    const W = 620, H = 280, padL = 56, padR = 24, padT = 24, padB = 36;
    const Nmin = 8, Nmax = 100;

    svg.innerHTML = '';
    // axes
    svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:padL,y1:H-padB,x2:W-padR,y2:H-padB,stroke:'var(--line)','stroke-width':1}));
    svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:padL,y1:padT,x2:padL,y2:H-padB,stroke:'var(--line)','stroke-width':1}));

    // pick a y-scale so that n^2/2 at n=Nmax is at the top
    const yMax = Nmax*Nmax/2;
    const xs = (n) => padL + (n - Nmin) / (Nmax - Nmin) * (W - padL - padR);
    const ys = (e) => (H - padB) - (e / yMax) * (H - padT - padB);

    // n^2/2 reference (the trivial Turán with r=∞)
    const dRef = [];
    for(let n=Nmin; n<=Nmax; n+=1) dRef.push(`${dRef.length?'L':'M'} ${xs(n).toFixed(2)} ${ys(n*n/2).toFixed(2)}`);
    svg.appendChild(SVG('path',{d:dRef.join(' '), fill:'none', stroke:'var(--mute)', 'stroke-width':1.2, 'stroke-dasharray':'4 4', opacity:0.7}));

    // KST upper bound: 0.5 * (t-1)^{1/s} * n^{2-1/s}
    const C = 0.5 * Math.pow(t-1, 1/s);
    const expo = 2 - 1/s;
    const dKst = [];
    for(let n=Nmin; n<=Nmax; n+=1){
      const y = C * Math.pow(n, expo);
      dKst.push(`${dKst.length?'L':'M'} ${xs(n).toFixed(2)} ${ys(y).toFixed(2)}`);
    }
    svg.appendChild(SVG('path',{d:dKst.join(' '), fill:'none', stroke:'var(--cyan)', 'stroke-width':2.4}));

    // Turán reference for r=2 (the K_3 case): n^2/4
    const dT2 = [];
    for(let n=Nmin; n<=Nmax; n+=1) dT2.push(`${dT2.length?'L':'M'} ${xs(n).toFixed(2)} ${ys(n*n/4).toFixed(2)}`);
    svg.appendChild(SVG('path',{d:dT2.join(' '), fill:'none', stroke:'var(--yellow)', 'stroke-width':1.6, 'stroke-dasharray':'2 5', opacity:0.85}));

    // axis labels
    const axx = SVG('text',{x:(W-padL-padR)/2 + padL, y:H-8, 'text-anchor':'middle','font-size':12, fill:'var(--mute)'});
    axx.textContent = 'n (vertices)';
    svg.appendChild(axx);
    const axy = SVG('text',{x:14, y:padT + (H-padT-padB)/2, 'text-anchor':'middle','font-size':12, fill:'var(--mute)', transform:`rotate(-90 14 ${padT + (H-padT-padB)/2})`});
    axy.textContent = 'edges (upper bound)';
    svg.appendChild(axy);

    // tick labels
    [Nmin, 30, 60, 100].forEach(n=>{
      const t = SVG('text',{x:xs(n), y:H-padB+14,'text-anchor':'middle','font-size':10, fill:'var(--mute)'});
      t.textContent = String(n);
      svg.appendChild(t);
    });

    // legend
    const legX = padL + 12, legY = padT + 10;
    const items = [
      ['var(--cyan)',   `KST: ½·(t−1)^{1/s}·n^{${expo.toFixed(3)}}`],
      ['var(--yellow)', `Turán K₃: n²/4`],
      ['var(--mute)',   `trivial: n²/2`]
    ];
    items.forEach((it, i)=>{
      svg.appendChild(SVG('rect',{x:legX, y:legY + i*16, width:14, height:3, fill:it[0]}));
      const t = SVG('text',{x:legX + 20, y:legY + i*16 + 5,'font-size':11, fill:'var(--ink)'});
      t.textContent = it[1];
      svg.appendChild(t);
    });

    out.textContent =
      `s = ${s},  t = ${t}\n` +
      `KST upper bound: ex(n, K_{s,t}) ≤ ${C.toFixed(3)} · n^{${expo.toFixed(3)}} + O(n)\n` +
      `at n = 100:   bound = ${(C*Math.pow(100,expo)).toFixed(0)} edges\n` +
      `compare:    n²/4 = 2500    n²/2 = 5000\n` +
      `as s → ∞ the exponent 2 − 1/s approaches 2 but never equals it.`;
  }
  sIn.addEventListener('input', render);
  tIn.addEventListener('input', render);
  render();
})();
</script>
</section>

3. Erdős–Stone–Simonovits

What if we forbid an arbitrary graph $H$? The answer involves only one number: the chromatic number $\chi(H)$.

Theorem (Erdős–Stone, 1946; Erdős–Simonovits, 1966). For any graph $H$ with at least one edge, $$\mathrm{ex}(n, H) \;=\; \Big(1 - \tfrac{1}{\chi(H) - 1}\Big)\,\frac{n^2}{2} \;+\; o(n^2).$$ Equivalently, $\pi(H) := \lim_{n \to \infty} \mathrm{ex}(n, H) / \binom{n}{2} = 1 - 1/(\chi(H) - 1)$.

What this says

The leading-order Turán density depends only on the chromatic number — not on the structure, not even on the number of vertices of $H$. Forbidding $K_4$ gives the same density as forbidding the Petersen graph (both have $\chi = 4$), namely $2/3$. Two consequences:

  • Bipartite case ($\chi(H) = 2$). The leading term is $0 \cdot n^2/2 = 0$, so $\mathrm{ex}(n, H) = o(n^2)$. The whole game becomes determining the lower-order rate — KST exponents, $C_{2k}$ exponents, and the celebrated Sidorenko conjecture ($\mathrm{ex}(n, H) \le \mathrm{ex}(n, \text{star})$ for bipartite $H$) live here.
  • Non-bipartite case. The Turán graph $T(n, \chi(H) - 1)$ is asymptotically extremal: it has chromatic number $\chi(H) - 1$, so it is $H$-free, and it has the right edge count.

Proof sketch — embedding via supersaturation

The lower bound is exhibited by $T(n, \chi(H) - 1)$. The upper bound: if $G$ has more than $(1 - 1/(\chi(H)-1))\,n^2/2 + \varepsilon n^2$ edges, then $G$ contains many copies of $K_{\chi(H), \chi(H), \ldots, \chi(H)}$ (the balanced complete multipartite with one part per colour class of $H$), into which $H$ embeds. The supersaturation step — "many edges $\Rightarrow$ many copies, not just one" — is the workhorse.

Turán density vs $\chi(H)$
step the chromatic number to read off the density curve
χ(H) = 3
For each $\chi$, the Turán density $\pi(H) = 1 - 1/(\chi-1)$ is the height of the dot. Below at $\chi=2$, the density is $0$ — bipartite forbidden subgraphs are $o(n^2)$, the regime of KST. As $\chi \to \infty$, $\pi \to 1$ — every graph eventually appears as a subgraph of any sufficiently dense host.

Used in
Extremal combinatorics · Triangle removal lemma and Szemerédi regularity

<script>
/* ============================ widget 3: Erdős–Stone density ============================ */
(function(){
  const chiIn = $('#es-chi');
  const chiv = $('#es-chiv');
  const svg = $('#es-svg'), out = $('#es-out');

  const examples = {
    2: 'bipartite (e.g. $C_4$, $K_{2,2}$, trees)',
    3: 'triangle $K_3$, $C_5$',
    4: '$K_4$, Petersen graph',
    5: '$K_5$',
    6: '$K_6$',
    7: '$K_7$',
    8: '$K_8$'
  };

  function render(){
    const chi = parseInt(chiIn.value,10);
    chiv.textContent = `χ(H) = ${chi}`;

    const W = 620, H = 300, padL = 56, padR = 24, padT = 24, padB = 40;
    const xs = c => padL + (c - 2) / (8 - 2) * (W - padL - padR);
    const ys = pi => (H - padB) - pi * (H - padT - padB);

    svg.innerHTML = '';
    // axes
    svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:padL,y1:H-padB,x2:W-padR,y2:H-padB,stroke:'var(--line)','stroke-width':1}));
    svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:padL,y1:padT,x2:padL,y2:H-padB,stroke:'var(--line)','stroke-width':1}));

    // density curve
    const pts = [];
    for(let c=2; c<=8; c++){
      const pi = c===2 ? 0 : 1 - 1/(c-1);
      pts.push({c, pi});
    }
    const path = pts.map((p,i)=>`${i?'L':'M'} ${xs(p.c).toFixed(2)} ${ys(p.pi).toFixed(2)}`).join(' ');
    svg.appendChild(SVG('path',{d:path, fill:'none', stroke:'var(--cyan)', 'stroke-width':2}));

    // dots and labels
    for(const p of pts){
      const isCur = p.c === chi;
      svg.appendChild(SVG('circle',{cx:xs(p.c), cy:ys(p.pi), r: isCur?7:4, fill: isCur?'var(--yellow)':'var(--cyan)', stroke:'var(--panel2)','stroke-width':1.2}));
      const lbl = SVG('text',{x:xs(p.c), y:ys(p.pi)-12, 'text-anchor':'middle','font-size':11, fill:'var(--mute)'});
      lbl.textContent = p.c===2 ? '0' : `${p.pi.toFixed(3)}`;
      svg.appendChild(lbl);
    }

    // x-axis chi labels
    for(let c=2; c<=8; c++){
      const t = SVG('text',{x:xs(c), y:H-padB+16,'text-anchor':'middle','font-size':11, fill:'var(--mute)'});
      t.textContent = `χ=${c}`;
      svg.appendChild(t);
    }
    // y-axis ticks
    [0, 0.5, 1].forEach(v=>{
      const yy = ys(v);
      svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:padL-4, y1:yy, x2:padL, y2:yy, stroke:'var(--mute)','stroke-width':1}));
      const t = SVG('text',{x:padL-8, y:yy+4,'text-anchor':'end','font-size':10, fill:'var(--mute)'});
      t.textContent = v.toFixed(1);
      svg.appendChild(t);
    });
    const axy = SVG('text',{x:18, y:padT + (H-padT-padB)/2, 'text-anchor':'middle','font-size':12, fill:'var(--mute)', transform:`rotate(-90 18 ${padT + (H-padT-padB)/2})`});
    axy.textContent = 'Turán density π(H)';
    svg.appendChild(axy);

    const pi = chi===2 ? 0 : 1 - 1/(chi-1);
    out.textContent =
      `χ(H) = ${chi}    typical example: ${examples[chi]}\n` +
      `Turán density π(H) = ${chi===2?'0   (bipartite — sub-quadratic, see KST §2)':`1 − 1/${chi-1} = ${pi.toFixed(4)}`}\n` +
      `extremal example: T(n, ${chi-1})    (complete ${chi-1}-partite, balanced)\n` +
      `at n = 1000: ex(n, H) ≈ ${(pi * 1000*1000/2).toFixed(0)} edges`;
  }
  chiIn.addEventListener('input', render);
  render();
})();
</script>
</section>

4. Ramsey numbers

Turán-type theorems forbid one specific subgraph globally; Ramsey-type theorems insist that any partition produces a forbidden monochromatic copy. The slogan: complete disorder is impossible.

Ramsey's theorem (graph form). For every $k, \ell \ge 2$ there exists a smallest integer $R(k, \ell)$ such that every $2$-edge-coloring of $K_{R(k,\ell)}$ contains a monochromatic $K_k$ in colour $1$ or a monochromatic $K_\ell$ in colour $2$.

The numbers $R(k, \ell)$ — and their diagonal versions $R(k) := R(k, k)$ — are notoriously hard to compute. Only a handful of exact values are known.

$R(3, \ell)$$R(k, k)$
$3$$R(3,3) = 6$$R(3,3) = 6$
$4$$R(3,4) = 9$$R(4,4) = 18$
$5$$R(3,5) = 14$$43 \le R(5,5) \le 46$ (open)
$6$$R(3,6) = 18$$102 \le R(6,6) \le 161$ (open)
$\Theta$$R(3, \ell) = \Theta(\ell^2 / \log \ell)$$\sqrt{2}^k \le R(k,k) \le 4^k$

$R(3, 3) = 6$

The lower bound $R(3, 3) > 5$ is exhibited by the $5$-cycle $C_5$ together with its complement (also $C_5$) — neither contains a triangle. The upper bound $R(3, 3) \le 6$ is the pigeonhole gem: in any $2$-coloring of $K_6$, fix a vertex $v$; among its $5$ incident edges, three share a colour (say red); those three endpoints either form a red triangle (with $v$) or include a blue edge, which closes a blue triangle.

The probabilistic lower bound

Erdős (1947) proved $R(k, k) \ge (1 + o(1))\frac{k}{e \sqrt{2}} 2^{k/2}$ by colouring $K_n$ uniformly at random. The expected number of monochromatic $K_k$s is $\binom{n}{k} \cdot 2^{1 - \binom{k}{2}}$; if this is less than $1$ then with positive probability no monochromatic clique exists, so $R(k,k) > n$. This was the founding example of the probabilistic method: an existence proof using only an inequality on a random structure.

Schur's theorem & sum-free sets

An arithmetic Ramsey relative: for every $r$, there is $S(r)$ such that any $r$-colouring of $\{1, \ldots, S(r)\}$ contains a monochromatic solution to $x + y = z$. Equivalently, $\{1, \ldots, n\}$ cannot be partitioned into $r$ sum-free sets (those containing no $x + y = z$) once $n \ge S(r)$. Proof: apply graph Ramsey to the colouring of edges of $K_n$ by $c(\{i, j\}) := $ colour-of$(|i - j|)$; a monochromatic $K_3$ on $\{a < b < c\}$ gives the equation $(c-b) + (b-a) = (c-a)$ with the same colour on all three differences.

The exponential gap. The diagonal Ramsey bounds $\sqrt{2}^k \le R(k,k) \le 4^k$ have only been improved by lower-order factors since 1947 — until 2023, when Campos, Griffiths, Morris, and Sahasrabudhe proved $R(k,k) \le (4 - \varepsilon)^k$ for an explicit $\varepsilon > 0$. The lower bound is still essentially Erdős's. Bridging this gap is one of the great unsolved problems.

$R(3, 3) = 6$ — colour the edges
click an edge to flip its colour; can you avoid both a red $K_3$ and a blue $K_3$?
The $5$-vertex case admits the $C_5$ colouring (red cycle, blue chords) with no monochromatic triangle — proving $R(3,3) > 5$. Switch to $K_6$ and try; you will fail. Triangles found are listed in the readout.

<script>
/* ============================ widget 4: R(3,3) explorer ============================ */
(function(){
  const svg = $('#ramsey-svg'), out = $('#ramsey-out');
  const btnClear = $('#ramsey-clear'), btnC5 = $('#ramsey-c5'), chk6 = $('#ramsey-n6');

  let n = 5;            // vertices
  let coloring = {};    // 'i-j' -> 0 (red) or 1 (blue)
  function key(i,j){ return i<j? `${i}-${j}` : `${j}-${i}`; }

  function reset(){
    coloring = {};
    for(let i=0;i<n;i++) for(let j=i+1;j<n;j++) coloring[key(i,j)] = 0; // all red
  }
  reset();

  function loadC5(){
    n = 5; chk6.checked = false;
    coloring = {};
    // C_5 edges = (0,1),(1,2),(2,3),(3,4),(4,0) red; chords blue
    for(let i=0;i<n;i++) for(let j=i+1;j<n;j++){
      const cyclic = (j === i+1) || (i===0 && j===n-1);
      coloring[key(i,j)] = cyclic ? 0 : 1;
    }
  }

  function vertices(){
    const cx = 310, cy = 180, R = 130;
    const vs = [];
    for(let i=0;i<n;i++){
      const t = -Math.PI/2 + 2*Math.PI*i/n;
      vs.push({x: cx + R*Math.cos(t), y: cy + R*Math.sin(t)});
    }
    return vs;
  }

  function findMonoTriangles(){
    const reds = [], blues = [];
    for(let i=0;i<n;i++) for(let j=i+1;j<n;j++) for(let k=j+1;k<n;k++){
      const a = coloring[key(i,j)], b = coloring[key(j,k)], c = coloring[key(i,k)];
      if(a===0 && b===0 && c===0) reds.push([i+1,j+1,k+1]);
      if(a===1 && b===1 && c===1) blues.push([i+1,j+1,k+1]);
    }
    return {reds, blues};
  }

  function render(){
    svg.innerHTML = '';
    const vs = vertices();
    const {reds, blues} = findMonoTriangles();

    // edges
    for(let i=0;i<n;i++) for(let j=i+1;j<n;j++){
      const k = key(i,j);
      const colour = coloring[k]===0 ? 'var(--pink)' : 'var(--blue)';
      // visible line
      const ln = SVG('line',{x1:vs[i].x, y1:vs[i].y, x2:vs[j].x, y2:vs[j].y, stroke:colour, 'stroke-width':3, opacity:0.92});
      svg.appendChild(ln);
      // hit area
      const hit = SVG('line',{x1:vs[i].x, y1:vs[i].y, x2:vs[j].x, y2:vs[j].y, stroke:'transparent','stroke-width':14});
      hit.style.cursor = 'pointer';
      hit.addEventListener('click', ()=>{
        coloring[k] = 1 - coloring[k];
        render();
      });
      svg.appendChild(hit);
    }
    // vertices
    for(let i=0;i<n;i++){
      svg.appendChild(SVG('circle',{cx:vs[i].x, cy:vs[i].y, r:14, fill:'var(--panel2)', stroke:'var(--ink)','stroke-width':1.6}));
      const t = SVG('text',{x:vs[i].x, y:vs[i].y+5, 'text-anchor':'middle','font-size':13,'font-weight':600});
      t.textContent = String(i+1);
      svg.appendChild(t);
    }

    // readout
    const lines = [];
    lines.push(`vertices: K_${n}    edges: ${n*(n-1)/2}`);
    lines.push(`red triangles:  ${reds.length}    ${reds.slice(0,4).map(t=>`{${t.join(',')}}`).join('  ')}${reds.length>4?'  …':''}`);
    lines.push(`blue triangles: ${blues.length}    ${blues.slice(0,4).map(t=>`{${t.join(',')}}`).join('  ')}${blues.length>4?'  …':''}`);
    if(reds.length === 0 && blues.length === 0){
      lines.push('');
      lines.push(`✓ no monochromatic triangle.    R(3,3) > ${n}.`);
    } else {
      lines.push('');
      lines.push(`a monochromatic K_3 exists.    K_${n} cannot escape.`);
    }
    out.textContent = lines.join('\n');

    // legend
    svg.appendChild(SVG('rect',{x:14, y:14, width:14, height:6, fill:'var(--pink)'}));
    const lp = SVG('text',{x:34, y:22,'font-size':12, fill:'var(--ink)'});
    lp.textContent = 'red';
    svg.appendChild(lp);
    svg.appendChild(SVG('rect',{x:80, y:14, width:14, height:6, fill:'var(--blue)'}));
    const lb = SVG('text',{x:100, y:22,'font-size':12, fill:'var(--ink)'});
    lb.textContent = 'blue';
    svg.appendChild(lb);
    const tip = SVG('text',{x:14, y:H-12,'font-size':11, fill:'var(--mute)'});
    tip.textContent = 'click an edge to toggle its colour';
    svg.appendChild(tip);
  }
  const H = 380;

  btnClear.addEventListener('click', ()=>{ for(const k in coloring) coloring[k]=0; render(); });
  btnC5.addEventListener('click', ()=>{ loadC5(); render(); });
  chk6.addEventListener('change', ()=>{ n = chk6.checked ? 6 : 5; reset(); render(); });
  render();
})();

5. Sperner's theorem & the LYM inequality

Switch from graphs to set systems. The Boolean lattice $2^{[n]}$ has $2^n$ subsets of $[n] = \{1, \ldots, n\}$, ordered by inclusion. An antichain is a family $\mathcal{F} \subseteq 2^{[n]}$ no member of which contains another. How big can an antichain be?

Theorem (Sperner, 1928). For every $n \ge 1$, $$\max\{|\mathcal{F}| : \mathcal{F} \subseteq 2^{[n]}\ \text{is an antichain}\} \;=\; \binom{n}{\lfloor n/2 \rfloor}.$$ The extremal antichains are exactly the middle layer $\binom{[n]}{\lfloor n/2 \rfloor}$ (and, for odd $n$, the upper-middle layer).

The LYM inequality

Sperner is a corollary of the LYM inequality (Lubell, Yamamoto, Meshalkin, Bollobás), which is itself a counting argument over maximal chains in the Boolean lattice. For an antichain $\mathcal{F}$ in $2^{[n]}$, $$\sum_{F \in \mathcal{F}} \frac{1}{\binom{n}{|F|}} \;\le\; 1.$$

Proof. A maximal chain $\emptyset = C_0 \subset C_1 \subset \cdots \subset C_n = [n]$ is determined by a permutation of $[n]$: there are $n!$ of them. A given $F$ with $|F| = k$ lies on exactly $k!\,(n-k)! = n! / \binom{n}{k}$ chains. Since $\mathcal{F}$ is an antichain, no chain contains two elements of $\mathcal{F}$, so summing over $F \in \mathcal{F}$, $$\sum_{F \in \mathcal{F}} \frac{n!}{\binom{n}{|F|}} \;\le\; n!,$$ giving LYM. Sperner now follows: each summand is at least $1 / \binom{n}{\lfloor n/2 \rfloor}$, so $|\mathcal{F}| \le \binom{n}{\lfloor n/2 \rfloor}$.

BLYM and Bollobás's set-pair

The "B" in BLYM is Bollobás's strengthening: for any antichain $\mathcal{F}$ with $a_k = |\{F \in \mathcal{F} : |F| = k\}|$, one has $\sum_k a_k / \binom{n}{k} \le 1$. The same chain-counting works with weights, and yields the more general two-family theorem: if $A_1, \ldots, A_m$ and $B_1, \ldots, B_m$ are sets with $A_i \cap B_i = \emptyset$ but $A_i \cap B_j \ne \emptyset$ for $i \ne j$, then $\sum_i 1/\binom{|A_i|+|B_i|}{|A_i|} \le 1$. This bookkeeping argument fuels much of probabilistic combinatorics.

<script>
/* ============================ widget 5: Sperner ============================ */
(function(){
  const svg = $('#sperner-svg'), out = $('#sperner-out');
  const nIn = $('#sperner-n'), nv = $('#sperner-nv');
  const btnMid = $('#sperner-mid'), btnClear = $('#sperner-clear');
  let n = 4;
  let picks = new Set(); // mask -> picked

  function bits(mask){ const xs=[]; for(let i=0;i<n;i++) if(mask&(1<<i)) xs.push(i+1); return xs; }
  function fmt(mask){ const xs = bits(mask); return xs.length? '{'+xs.join(',')+'}' : '∅'; }
  function popcount(m){ let c=0; while(m){ c+=m&1; m>>=1; } return c; }
  function comb(n,k){ if(k<0||k>n) return 0; let r=1; for(let i=0;i<k;i++){ r = r*(n-i)/(i+1); } return Math.round(r); }
  function isSubset(a,b){ return (a & b) === a; }

  function render(){
    svg.innerHTML = '';
    const W = 620, H = 360, padT = 28, padB = 28;
    const ranks = []; for(let k=0;k<=n;k++) ranks.push([]);
    for(let m=0;m<(1<<n);m++) ranks[popcount(m)].push(m);
    const rowH = (H - padT - padB) / (n+1);

    // detect violations
    const picked = [...picks];
    const bad = new Set();
    for(let i=0;i<picked.length;i++) for(let j=0;j<picked.length;j++){
      if(i===j) continue;
      if(picked[i] !== picked[j] && isSubset(picked[i], picked[j])){
        bad.add(picked[i]); bad.add(picked[j]);
      }
    }

    // draw rank rows
    for(let k=0;k<=n;k++){
      const items = ranks[k];
      const y = padT + (n - k) * rowH;
      // rank label
      const lbl = SVG('text',{x:14, y:y+5, 'font-size':11, fill:'var(--mute)'});
      lbl.textContent = `rank ${k}: ${comb(n,k)}`;
      svg.appendChild(lbl);
      const xstart = 90, xend = W-30;
      const slot = (xend - xstart) / Math.max(1, items.length+1);
      items.forEach((m, idx) => {
        const x = xstart + slot*(idx+1);
        const isPick = picks.has(m);
        const isBad = bad.has(m);
        const color = isPick ? (isBad ? 'var(--pink)' : 'var(--yellow)') : 'var(--panel2)';
        const stroke = isPick ? (isBad ? 'var(--pink)' : 'var(--yellow)') : 'var(--line)';
        const w = 70, h = rowH * 0.72;
        const g = SVG('g',{});
        const r = SVG('rect',{x:x-w/2, y:y-h/2, width:w, height:h, fill:color, stroke, 'stroke-width':1.4, rx:5, opacity: isPick?0.95:0.6});
        r.style.cursor = 'pointer';
        r.addEventListener('click', ()=>{
          if(picks.has(m)) picks.delete(m); else picks.add(m);
          render();
        });
        g.appendChild(r);
        const t = SVG('text',{x, y:y+4, 'text-anchor':'middle', 'font-size':11, fill: isPick? '#111':'var(--ink)'});
        t.textContent = fmt(m);
        t.style.pointerEvents = 'none';
        g.appendChild(t);
        svg.appendChild(g);
      });
    }

    // LYM sum
    let sum = 0;
    for(const m of picks){ const k = popcount(m); sum += 1 / comb(n, k); }

    const sperner = comb(n, Math.floor(n/2));
    const lines = [];
    lines.push(`n = ${n}    Sperner bound: ⌊n/2⌋-layer has ${sperner} elements`);
    lines.push(`picked: ${picks.size} subsets`);
    lines.push(`LYM sum  Σ 1/C(n, |F|) = ${sum.toFixed(4)}    ${sum<=1.0+1e-9 ? '≤ 1 ✓ (LYM holds)' : '> 1 ✗ (impossible — these CANNOT all be antichain)'}`);
    if(bad.size === 0 && picks.size > 0) lines.push(`✓ antichain confirmed — no two picks are nested`);
    else if(bad.size > 0) lines.push(`✗ ${bad.size/2|0} nested pair(s) — pink boxes violate the antichain property`);
    out.textContent = lines.join('\n');
  }

  function loadMiddle(){
    picks.clear();
    const k = Math.floor(n/2);
    for(let m=0; m<(1<<n); m++) if(popcount(m) === k) picks.add(m);
    render();
  }
  nIn.addEventListener('input', ()=>{
    n = parseInt(nIn.value, 10);
    nv.textContent = `n = ${n}`;
    picks.clear();
    render();
  });
  btnMid.addEventListener('click', loadMiddle);
  btnClear.addEventListener('click', ()=>{ picks.clear(); render(); });
  render();
})();
</script>

6. The triangle removal lemma & Szemerédi regularity

The deepest tool of modern extremal graph theory. The triangle removal lemma says: a graph that is "almost" triangle-free is in fact "almost" a triangle-free graph after deleting a small number of edges.

Theorem (Ruzsa–Szemerédi triangle removal lemma). For every $\varepsilon > 0$ there exists $\delta = \delta(\varepsilon) > 0$ such that every graph $G$ on $n$ vertices containing fewer than $\delta n^3$ triangles can be made triangle-free by deleting at most $\varepsilon n^2$ edges.

Why the contrapositive matters

The reformulation is potent: if $G$ contains at least one triangle and is "robust" (you must delete $\ge \varepsilon n^2$ edges to destroy all triangles), then it contains $\ge \delta n^3$ triangles — so triangles come in supersaturated packs. This is the recurring pattern in extremal combinatorics: a single forbidden copy compresses to a quadratic-density-many copies as a counting threshold.

Application: Roth's theorem on $3$-term progressions

The triangle removal lemma yields Roth's theorem (1953): every set $A \subseteq \{1, \ldots, N\}$ free of $3$-term arithmetic progressions has size $|A| = o(N)$. Sketch: build a tripartite graph on three copies of $\mathbb{Z}/N\mathbb{Z}$ with edges $(x, y)$ if $y - x \in A$, $(y, z)$ if $z - y \in A$, $(x, z)$ if $(z - x)/2 \in A$. Triangles correspond to $3$-APs in $A$; trivial triangles ($x=y=z$) number exactly $|A| \cdot N$, are edge-disjoint, and force the graph to be supersaturated — so by removal there is a non-trivial $3$-AP unless $|A|/N \to 0$.

Szemerédi regularity (the engine room)

Regularity Lemma (Szemerédi, 1975). For every $\varepsilon > 0$ there exists $K = K(\varepsilon)$ such that every graph $G$ on $n$ vertices admits a partition $V = V_0 \cup V_1 \cup \cdots \cup V_k$ with $k \le K$, $|V_0| \le \varepsilon n$, $|V_i| = |V_j|$ for $i, j \ge 1$, and all but $\varepsilon k^2$ pairs $(V_i, V_j)$ are $\varepsilon$-regular (their bipartite density is robust to taking large sub-pairs).

Translation: every dense graph looks, when viewed at the right resolution, like a quasi-random graph between roughly equal blobs. The bound $K(\varepsilon)$ is a tower of twos of height $\varepsilon^{-O(1)}$ — Gowers (1997) showed this is sharp. Counterintuitively, it is the partition, not the blocks, that does the work: a counting lemma converts the regularity decomposition into approximate copies of any fixed pattern.

The whole package. Regularity + Counting + Removal is the standard argumentative loop. Regularity decomposes $G$; the counting lemma estimates copies of a fixed $H$ in regular pairs; if $H$-copies are scarce in $G$, all "rich" pairs can be cleaned out, removing few edges and leaving an $H$-free graph. Roth, Szemerédi's theorem on long APs, the Green–Tao theorem on primes — all run on this loop.

slide $\varepsilon$ to see the implied $\delta$ frontier; toggle "robust" to see supersaturation

7. Connections

Extremal combinatorics asks "how big can an object with property $P$ be?". The bridges below are where the same question is answered by a different toolset.

Probabilistic existence proofs

The matching lower bounds for most extremal numbers come from the probabilistic method; a deterministic upper bound and a random lower bound are the basic shape of extremal arguments.

Spectral and algebraic methods

Spectral graph theory provides eigenvalue-based upper bounds (Hoffman, Alon–Boppana, Friedman) on Ramsey-type quantities; the polynomial method and finite-field arithmetic give field-theoretic upper bounds.

Simplicial and topological methods

Simplicial complexes appear via the Borsuk–Ulam-style topological lower bounds (Kneser–Lovász), where the chromatic number of a graph is bounded by the connectivity of its neighbourhood complex.

Matroid-style independence and duality

Matroid theory supplies the abstraction in which "independent set" / "matching" / "transversal" arguments are clean — Edmonds’s matroid intersection and union theorems are the structural backbone.

Open frontiers: the structure-vs-randomness dichotomy (Tao–Ziegler, Green–Tao), removal lemmas at every uniformity (regularity, hypergraph regularity, arithmetic regularity), and flag-algebra computer-assisted proofs of long-standing extremal conjectures.
